If you have any questions during, feel free to just like jump in and ask.

But we can kind of get us get started.
I'm going to share my screen.
So this is pretty much the Relay dashboard.
So when you log into the Relay dashboard, and let me give you some insight into maybe Relay as
Product, its purpose, and kind of what it's really meant to do and how it differentiates itself a little bit.
Relay is a financial tech company.
We provide financial services.
And essentially, the idea behind Relay is to really ensure that you have cash flow clarity for small to medium-sized business owners.

When we talk about cash flow clarity, we are the only platform that's backed by the profit-first method, which we'll touch a little bit on when we talk a little bit about some of the product features on the platform.

What I personally love about Relay is it has a lot of banking features that are really blended well with business finance features that a lot of business owners would probably want in a built-in kind of all-in-one platform.
And that suite is growing as we speak.
We're coming out with new product launches.
All the time as we gain kind of user feedback, right?
When you log into the Relay Dashboard, this is pretty much what you're brought to.
So you're brought to your homepage.
You'll see your name as well as your business name in the top right.
And you're given some information right off the bat with regards to the business itself.
Much like a lot of banking platforms, they're online portals.
So you'll be able to see right off the bat your total balance as well as your available balance.
You'll also be able to see your pending transactions here as well as any posted transactions on the platform that have successfully gone through and posted to the account.
Obviously, the difference between the total balance and the available balance is your pending transactions.
So if you have any holds on the account, for example, in this particular test environment, we have a hold for a check deposit that was a payment.
Payment to like a user or vendor or merchant that will be reflected here in your available balance versus your total balance.
You'll also have the option to move money or add money on the homepage.
We'll talk a little bit about that in literally a little bit when we get into the account section.
But when you click into your transaction, so I'm going to click into one, you'll have pretty much all of your transaction detail built into this page as well.
So obviously the amount, the payment timeline, right?
This is out of the way, sorry.
Payment timeline, transfer details.
If there's a receipt for expense management, you can add that here as well.
The balance that this brought it to, where it pulled the funds from, how it was initiated, even some geographical information here and memos that you can add as well, as well as your transaction ID.
So on the homepage, you get a little bit of information right off the bat, right?
regards to...
The financial flow and how much you have available balance.
But when we go into the account section, that's really where you get to get a more granular view of the cash flow income versus outgoing.
And we're going to explore the account section now a little bit more in depth.
This is going to give you, again, a more granular view.

So with regards to the account section on the Relay platform, this is where I would say a lot of business owners kind of live and where they get some real insight into cash flow clarity for their business.
Relay has the option to add on up to 20 checkings accounts as well as two savings accounts.
And there is no minimum requirement.
There's no fee with regards to a minimum balance whatsoever.
So that's certainly one of our differentiating points, I would say, as a platform.
Super.
Very exciting if you're somebody who's like really into Profit First, for example, or if you want to be able to kind of separate your expenses per account, you have the ability to do that.
So it certainly is a differentiator here.
In order to add an account, you can actually add an account fairly simply.
So you can just click this Add Account button.
You'll put in a nickname.
So for example, here I'm going to put Test.
You can select whether it's a checkings or a savings account, and you can select team member access.
We'll dive a little bit more into team members a little bit later, and then you'll click Create Account.
I'm not actually going to do it because this is a test environment, but you'll click Create Account, and it'll pretty much populate a little square with your account information right off the bat.
These are real bank accounts.

So if you were to select the account numbers by clicking this icon, you'll notice each account has its own individual account number, meaning that if you're receiving payments from clients, for example, you're...
We're actually able to differentiate which account they're paying to just by providing your account number and the routing number associated with that.
So again, really giving control over cash flow with regards to small to medium-sized businesses.
You can also reorder the accounts.
You can decide, I want my operating expense account first, for example, just so you have total control of how visually things are organized for you on this page as well.
When you click into your accounts, this will look very similar to the homepage, but it'll be specific to that account, right?
So here we're in the operating expenses account.
You'll have the available balance and the total balance for that particular account, as well as pending transactions and posted transactions here as well.
When you click on these transactions, it's the same type of information that you'll find on the homepage.
So again, payment, settlement, timeline, date, if you want to add a receipt, again, we'll talk a little bit about that.
Later when we get to expense management, geographical information, you also have the ability to search.
So if you want to search like a specific amount, you have that option, or you can add filters if you'd like as well, just so that you can pull up transaction information as quickly as possible.
Here, you can also download your statements.
You have the option to export your statements in PDF, CSV, or OFX file, and it's per month.
Obviously, this particular statement for September is going to be a partial statement because the month of September is over, right?
But you have the option to download multiple statements at a time.
And there's also the Details tab, which will give you your account details.
So your account number, your routing number, your domestic wire details, right?
So what information do you need to provide to receive a domestic wire, your international wire details as well, depending on the currency that you're receiving the fund.
And as well as team members that have access to this particular account information as well.
We're going to take a little bit of a pause.
Any questions with regards to anything I've covered so far?
This is kind of like the very basics of navigating.

Okay, awesome.

So you have the option to move money.
These are instant transfers in between accounts.
We're going to touch on payments and auto transfer rules in just a second, payments a little bit later.
But if you want to transfer money from one account to another, you can do that.
And these happen pretty much instantaneously.
So you'll select the account that you want the money transferred from.
You'll add the account that you want the money transferred to.
You can also select the transfer frequency.
So if you want it to be a one-time transfer, for example, or a reoccurring transfer, you can literally break it down to every minute.
Not going to judge you if you do every minute.
That's a little bit wild to me, but, you know, people have their businesses and they want to run it.
So fair.
You can set your internal memo as well for your own bookkeeping reasons, for example, or just as a reminder.
So fairly simple there.
You can also add money, right?

Where do I go from here, right?
And a lot of the time, the first step to really unlock all of the features on the Relay platform is to fund the account.
If you don't fund the account, you actually aren't able to set up auto transfer rules.
For example, you can't use your debit card, right?
So we normally recommend taking that as a first step.
Adding money is pretty simple.
You can receive it via domestic.
BasicWire, InternationalWire, or you can connect to an external bank using Plaid.
The workflow is fairly simple.
I'm not going to input my phone number right now, but you would input your phone number, and it's going to bring you to a page where you're essentially going to select your bank.
It'll prompt you to actually log in to your bank, your bank, and it'll essentially create that connection through Plaid, and you'll be able to actually transfer funds in.

And once you have an account that you want to transfer to selected, you'll also be able to see...
Your daily limit.
Keep in mind transfers in to the Relay platform via this method.
Take around two to three business days.
There's a couple of reasons behind that.
But the main reason is you have to be mindful that there are two financial institutions that have now connected via a third party.
There's a lot of compliance on both sides.
So the funding takes days to settle.
However, if you're like in a pickle, you can always do an ACH through your external financial institution.
And add the funds in by providing your routing and account number.

Yeah.

We found that 20 check-ins accounts was more than sufficient for most businesses.
Even businesses, for example, were very popular with the real estate industry.
People will have check-ins accounts for each individual property.
Larger clients of ours, 20 check-ins out accounts was more than sufficient, and obviously, two savings accounts was more than sufficient there as well.

can connect your external account through here.

So the auto transfer rules, again, we are like the platform that is backed by the profit first method.
How familiar are you with the Profit First method?
I just heard about it last week.
Okay, that's okay.
Yeah, it is a...
I don't know if it's a newer method per se, but it's certainly not something that a lot of people know about unless you're speaking to an accountant who has a background in Profit First.
So totally okay.
But Profit First, essentially, like the name suggests, is a way of managing your finances by putting your profit first.
So the idea is that you're allocating percentages of your income into different checkings accounts so that you have a more granular view of cash flow incoming versus outgoing, right?

So you would set a percentage target for Profit First, and then you would use the remaining percentage of 100% to dictate how your cash is moving through your business through those checkings accounts.
So...
So...
So we're going to look at the Profit First Rule.
There's another one called the Maximum Balance Rule.
To be really transparent, it's fairly similar, but the Profit First Rule is really what most users use.
I'm going to nickname this peanut butter fund.
I don't know.
Just to have an example.
So you're able to really allocate funds through these auto transfer rules.

So what this is going to do is it's essentially going to create a rule in the system that's going to allocate funds automatically based on a frequency that you set and how much you want to keep in your actual account that you're disbursing funds from.
So, for example, I'm going to set an auto transfer rule.
I'm going to do it every minute.
No, I'm not going to do it every minute.
I'm going to do it every month.
Maybe I'll do it on the...
Seventh, right, I'm going to keep $10 in my income account, and then I'm going to allocate the remaining funds into my other accounts.
So I'm going to do 50% to operating expenses, 30% to payroll, I'll do 15% to debt repayment, for example, and then maybe 5% to that profit account.
Now, this is pretty standard percentages.
So when you create an auto transfer rule, these percentages are actually auto populated, but you have the option, obviously, to edit this based on your business needs.

But when you're using the profit first method, this is kind of like the, not the golden rule, but this is kind of what they suggest starting with and then editing as you see that your business fluctuates or your business changes.
And then you can create that rule.
When you create the rule, it'll ask you to confirm.
Right, so the peanut butter fund.
It's going to do every month on the 7th.
It's going to keep $10 in the income account and transfer 50% to operating expenses, 30% to payroll, 15% to debt repayment, and 5% to profit.
It'll also tell you actually when the transfer will initiate at, so 2 a.m.
Eastern Standard Time, that's the time zone I live in, and when the first transfer will take place.
I'm, again, not going to actually create this rule because it's a test environment that many people use.
If they saw a random rule come up called the peanut butter fund, they might be like, who touched this?
So not going to do that.
The maximum balance rule is a little bit different.

So essentially what this is, is it is telling the system you want to keep a maximum balance in the account, and you're just going to transfer anything outside of that excess balance to another account.
So a little bit different, to be honest, it's not super, super different from the profit first rule, but if you're not someone who's doing profit first.
No.
And you want to keep a maximum balance in your account.
Anytime it goes over that maximum balance, it'll transfer the funds out into the allocated accounts that you have pretty much set up.
And then you'll see all of your auto transfer rules that are set up already.
So we can see there's one that's a weekly allocation as well as a debt fund, right?
So you'll see all of that here.
When you click into these, you can actually toggle them.
So you can turn them off at any time, turn them on.
And then when you click the three dots, you can edit or delete your auto transfer rule as well.
So it's never, ever, once you make an auto transfer rule, it's not like set in stone.
Again, right, people's businesses change.
So these things may need to change as the business grows.
So we've given users the option to obviously edit their auto transfer rules as their business grows.
Any questions about the account section?
Because we're pretty much done with the account section.

The card section is my favorite section.
I am obsessed with the card section.
Never in my 30 years of life did I ever say I would think that I would say something like that, that I would be obsessed with the card section in a banking tool.
But I think the developers did an amazing and the product team did an amazing job with the card section, the suite of tools you have in the card section is, in my opinion, top notch.
So we're going to pretty much dive right in.
So the card section, when you go into the card section, you're going to have a list of all of your cards here.
We'll talk a little bit about what you can do with this.
But we're going to first start off by creating a new card right off the bat.
When you create a new card, there's a couple of things that I think are really key differentiators.
Relay takes pride in, again, right, the idea is having cash flow clarity.
It's about having, you know, control over your funds and your cash flow for your business.
So when you set up a new card, you're going to select your card format.
You have the option to do a virtual card.
The virtual card has all of the features as a physical card.
However, obviously, it's virtual, so you won't physically have the card, but you can add this into your Apple wallet, your virtual wallet, for example.
Use it for online subscriptions.
The benefit of using the virtual card is it's activated and ready to use immediately, so there's no processing time or waiting time to get it activated.

So if you're in a pinch, let's say, and for whatever reason, your physical card isn't broken, you can literally, on the mobile app, create a virtual card, add it to your wallet, and pay at a restaurant, for example.
So super handy there.
For the purpose of this, we're going to create a virtual card.
You can also obviously create a physical card.
It gets mailed to

So by default, your business address, but you do have the option to change that when you go through the workflow, you can put in a custom address, or if you've assigned the card to a team member, you can input your team member's address as well.
When it's mailed, it takes four to 10 business days.
There's a bit of a gap in that because that timeline actually takes into account if you've chosen Express Shipping.
And I believe the fee for Express Shipping is around $12.
So if you choose Express Shipping, you'll get the card faster.
But if you go for free shipping, it'll take up to that 10 business day timeline.
Really cool features here.
So you'll have your source account.
That's not really exciting.
That's not what I necessarily was referencing.
So you're going to obviously select the account that the funds are going to get pulled from.
But one of the really cool features is the backup account.
So the backup account was really the idea behind the backup account.
We wanted to ensure that if you're at a store, you can avoid the looming decline messaging on a POS system, for example.
The embarrassment that comes with that as a person if you get declined at a store.
So the backup account, what it does is it does something called a fun sweep.
So let's say I'm at a gas station and I want to buy, I don't know, a Snickers bar, and I'm paying at the POS.
I've set up this virtual card.
I'm using my TAP, right?
It's going to pull money from the operating expenses account, and this Snickers bar is worth more than $101,000 for whatever reason.
That's how much they've set it up for.
It's worth $120,000.
I go to TAP.
It doesn't have enough funds to cover the Snickers bar.
What the system will then do is it'll pull from the backup account to do a fun sweep to cover the cost of the Snickers bar.
And then...
So that you don't get that decline message.
You don't have to set up a backup account, but if you're someone who uses cards really frequently or, you know, you want to avoid that kind of embarrassment, you do have that option.
I love this.
I think this is super cool.
You'll set up, obviously, your primary cardholder.
So I am going to choose my real name, which is not Sina.
It's Toby Tobias.
And then we're going to go on to the next page.
Which, this suite of tools here is the control card spend.
Again, the idea is to have cash flow clarity.
It's to be able to have control over your funds as a business owner.
So you have the option to set spending limits built into Relay.
So if I am giving this card to an employee of mine, for example, and I want to set a vendor restriction, right?
I want them to block.
All food and drink places.
I don't want them to be able to buy any food or drink, right?
So you can set up this category restriction to either block or allow, or you can get even more granular and you can select specific vendors, right?
So I don't want them to do bench accounting.
I don't want them to use this card for bench accounting for whatever reason.
So it really gives you control over how the card actually is being able to be used.
How this kind of came about is we got a lot of feedback from business owners in the construction sector.
And obviously they have a lot of contractors that work under them.
And we noticed through feedback that, you know, these contractors would go, they would get gas, right?
The, the business owner would give them a gas card and they would go to a gas station and they would literally just like buy a Snickers bar and a Snickers bar is fairly.
inexpensive.
But if you're doing that every day, that adds up as a business owner.
And if you have multiple contractors doing that every day, that adds up even more, right?
So we kind of built in this option so that you're able to really control where people spend.
You can also set spending limits, right?
So if you're like, I want this person to only be able to spend $10 a day, and I want to set a limit for the month for $300, right?
So you have that option to do that.
If it goes over the spending limit, it'll decline the card.
You can also put in pre-approved spending.
This is actually a newer feature, but essentially what it is, is it requires employees who have the card to get approval before they can actually use the card for a transaction.
So you can set it so that it approves all spending and you'll set it to whoever, let's pick Robin for, or you can set it for an amount
So this, I think it's probably what most people would use.
So if I'm a business owner, I want to know when someone is spending more than $1,000, and I trust Robin.
So I want Robin to be able to approve these types of transactions.
You also have access control.
So access control is essentially like if I'm a business owner located in the U.S., I don't really foresee myself leaving the U.S.
I don't foresee myself using the card overseas, I can block international transactions.
This is also an added security measure as well, right?
So if for whatever reason my card details get compromised, if it's overseas, it'll actually automatically block any transactions that happen overseas.
So again, this whole section is really about giving you control over how your funds are being used and where they're going.

Like, I have a card for food and I have a card for gas.
Like, it was difficult for them to be able to pick up a card and know which card was for what.

can give you your most recent transaction, and it'll list who the receipt admin is here.
